subject: Chemistry
q: Water does not produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> on reacting with :
question: Water does not produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CO</mi></math> on reacting with :
solution:
Carbon monoxide does not react with water to form an acid.

The carbon monoxide molecule is one that won't react with water at all. As the acidic oxide has a higher oxidation state, it reacts with water to produce acid, and reacts with base to produce salt. 

CO2+H2O→H2CO3

C+H2Osteam→CO+H2

CH4+H2O→CO+3H2C3H8+H2O→3CO+H2 both reactions are carried out at 1270 K temp. with Ni catalyst

subject: Chemistry
q:
In basic medium, H2O2 exhibits which of the following reactions?
(A) Mn2+→Mn4+
(B) I2→I-
(C) PbS→PbSO4
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
In basic medium, H2O2 exhibits which of the following reactions?
(A) Mn2+→Mn4+
(B) I2→I-
(C) PbS→PbSO4
Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution:
In basic medium, oxidising action of H2O2.

Mn2++H2O2→Mn+4+2OH-

In basic medium, reducing action of H2O2

I2+H2O2+2OH-→2I-+2H2O+O2

In acidic medium, oxidising action of H2O2

PbS(s)+4H2O2(aq)→PbSO4( s)+4H2O(l)

Hence correct option (4).

subject: Chemistry
q:
The mass of iron converted into $Fe_3O_4$ by the action of $18$ g of steam is :
(Given : Molar mass of H, O and Fe are $1$, $16$ and $56$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
Assume iron is present in excess :
question:
The mass of iron converted into $Fe_3O_4$ by the action of $18$ g of steam is :
(Given : Molar mass of H, O and Fe are $1$, $16$ and $56$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
Assume iron is present in excess :
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for the reaction of iron with steam is:

$3Fe + 4H_2O \rightarrow Fe_3O_4 + 4H_2$

Molar mass of $H_2O = 2 \times 1 + 16 = 18 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

Number of moles of steam ($H_2O$) given = $\dfrac{18}{18} = 1 \text{ mol}$

From the balanced equation, $4 \text{ moles}$ of $H_2O$ react with $3 \text{ moles}$ of $Fe$.

Therefore, $1 \text{ mole}$ of $H_2O$ will react with $\dfrac{3}{4} \text{ moles}$ of $Fe$.

Mass of iron reacted = Moles of $Fe \times$ Molar mass of $Fe$

Mass of iron = $\dfrac{3}{4} \times 56 = 3 \times 14 = 42 \text{ g}$

Answer: $42$ g

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Physical properties of isotopes of hydrogen are different.

Reason R : Mass difference between isotopes of hydrogen is very large.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Physical properties of isotopes of hydrogen are different.

Reason R : Mass difference between isotopes of hydrogen is very large.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
The isotopes of hydrogen are protium, deuterium, and tritium, which have different physical properties. For example, deuterium has a higher boiling point and a lower vapor pressure than protium, while tritium is radioactive and has a much shorter half-life than the other two isotopes.

The mass difference between the isotopes of hydrogen is indeed significant, with protium having a mass of 1 atomic mass unit (amu), deuterium having a mass of 2 amu, and tritium having a mass of 3 amu.

So Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

q: An indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ is used for studying the effect of variation in concentration of iodide on the rate of reaction of iodide ion with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at room temp. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ forms blue colored complex with compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ present in the solution. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ and compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ respectively are
question: An indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ is used for studying the effect of variation in concentration of iodide on the rate of reaction of iodide ion with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> at room temp. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ forms blue colored complex with compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ present in the solution. The indicator ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>’ and compound ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>’ respectively are
solution:
The reaction between iodide ions and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) occurs in the acidic medium and can be represented in the following manner:

2I- (aq) + H2O2 (l) + 2H+ (aq) → I2 (g) + 2H2O (l)

In this reaction, hydrogen peroxide oxidises iodide ions (I-) to molecular iodine. If a calculated amount of sodium thiosulphate is added in the presence of starch solution as an indicator to the above reaction mixture, the liberated iodine reacts with thiosulphate ions as fast as it is formed and is reduced back to iodide ions till all the thiosulphate ions are oxidised to tetrathionate ions.

I2 (g) + 2S2O32- (aq) → S4O62- (aq) + 2I- (aq)

After the complete consumption of thiosulphate ions, the concentration of iodine liberated in the reaction of hydrogen peroxide with iodide ions increases rapidly to a point where iodine forms intense blue complex with starch.

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of isotopes of hydrogen and number of radioactive isotopes among them, respectively, are
question: The total number of isotopes of hydrogen and number of radioactive isotopes among them, respectively, are
solution:
Hydrogen is the first element in the periodic table and has the atomic number one. Those elements which have the same atomic number but a different mass number are called isotopes. There are three isotopes of hydrogen namely, protium H11, deuterium H12 or D and lastly tritium H13 or T. The isotopes are different because of the different number of neutrons present in them.

Isotopes of Hydrogen:



Among all three isotopes only Tritium is radioactive.

solution: H2O2 decomposes on exposure to sunlight or on heating. It also decomposes on standing but at a slow rate. These reactions occur explosively when H2O2 is in contact with dust. Urea acts as a negative catalyst to this reaction and slow down the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.

q: Hydrogen has three isotopes : protium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>1</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math>, deuterium (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>2</mn></mmultiscripts></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>) and tritium (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>3</mn></mmultiscripts></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>). They have nearly same chemical properties but different physical properties. They differ in
question: Hydrogen has three isotopes : protium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>1</mn></mmultiscripts></mfenced></math>, deuterium (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>2</mn></mmultiscripts></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>) and tritium (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>3</mn></mmultiscripts></math> or <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>). They have nearly same chemical properties but different physical properties. They differ in
solution:
Isotopes are the atoms of the same element with different mass number. They have the same atomic number. So, number of protons and electrons are same, Hence their electronic configuration are same, but number of neutrons are different.

Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium, H11, deuterium, H12 or D and tritium, H13 or T.  These isotopes differ from one another in respect of the presence of neutrons. Ordinary hydrogen, protium, has no neutrons, deuterium (also known as heavy hydrogen) has one and tritium has two neutrons in the nucleus.

question:
Given below are two reactions, involved in the commercial production of dihydrogen H2. The two reactions are carried out at temperature “T1” and “T2”, respectively

Cs+H2Og⟶T1COg+H2g

COg+H2OgT2CatalystCO2g+H2g

The temperatures T1 and T2 are correctly related as
solution:
Cs+H2Og⟶1270 K T1COg+H2g is known as coal gasification reaction. The mixture produced in this reaction is called water gas.

COg+H2Og673 K T2Catalyst→CO2g+H2g is known as water gas shift reaction. Using water gas shift reaction we can increase the concentration of hydrogen gas.

Hence, T1>T2

solution:

2KMnO4+3H2SO4+5H2O2→K2SO4+2MnSO4+8H2O+5O2

In the above reaction, potassium permanganate acts an oxidising agent.

subject: Chemistry
q: Hydrogen peroxide acts both as an oxidising and as a reducing agent depending upon the nature of the reacting species. In which of the following cases $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ acts as a reducing agent in acid medium?
question: Hydrogen peroxide acts both as an oxidising and as a reducing agent depending upon the nature of the reacting species. In which of the following cases $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ acts as a reducing agent in acid medium?
solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ acts as a reducing agent only in presence of strong oxidising agents (i.e., $\left.\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}\right)$in acidic as well as alkaline medium.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&2 \mathrm{KMnO}_4+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \\
&\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+2 \mathrm{MnSO}_4+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+5 \mathrm{O}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements about water is FALSE?
question: Which of the following statements about water is FALSE?
options: There is extensive intramolecular hydrogen bonding in the condensed phase.
Ice formed by heavy water sinks in normal water.
Water is oxidized to oxygen during photosynthesis
Water can act both as an acid and as a base
solution:
(i) Ice formed by heavy water sinks in normal water due to higher density of D2O than normal water.

(ii) 6 CO 2 + 6 H 2 O → chlorophyll hv C 6 H 12 O 6 +6O 2

(iii) Water can show amphiprotic nature and hence water can act both as an acid a base.

(iv) There is extensive intermolecular hydrogen bonding in the condensed phase instead of intramolecular H - bonding.

question: Which one of the following statements is incorrect ?
options: Atomic hydrogen is produced when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> molecules at a high temperature are irradiated with UV radiation.

At around <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the dissociation of dihydrogen into its atoms is nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>%</mo></math>

Bond dissociation enthalpy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is highest among diatomic gaseous molecules which contain a single bond.

Dihydrogen is produced on reacting zinc with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> as well as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NaOH</mi><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi>aq</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></mrow></msub></math>.
solution: Atomic hydrogen is produced at high temperature in an electric arc or under ultraviolet radiations. The dissociation of dihydrogen at 2000 K is only 0.081%. H–H bond dissociation enthalpy is highest for a single bond for any diatomic molecule. Dihydrogen can be produced on reacting Zn with dil. HCl as well as NaOH (aq.).

subject: Chemistry
q: In which of the following reactions the hydrogen peroxide acts as a reducing agent?
question: In which of the following reactions the hydrogen peroxide acts as a reducing agent?
solution:
PbS+4H2O2→PbSO4+4H2O

In the above reaction lead sulphide oxidised to lead sulphate. Hence, hydrogen peroxide acts as oxidising agent.

2Fe2++H2O2→2Fe3++2OH-

In the above reaction Fe2+ oxidised to Fe3+. Hence, hydrogen peroxide acts as oxidising agent.

Mn2++H2O2→Mn4++2OH-

In the above reaction Mn2+ oxidised to Mn4+. Hence, hydrogen peroxide acts as oxidising agent.

HOCl+H2O2→H3O++Cl-1+O2

In the above reaction Cl+1 reduced to Cl-, hence, hydrogen peroxide acts as a reducing agent.
